questions.q: A line passes through the points whose position vectors are $\hat{i}+\hat{j}-2 \hat{k}$ and $\hat{i}-3 \hat{j}+\hat{k}$. The position vector of a point on it at a unit distance from the first point is/are
questions.source: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced
questions.q: If three planes $P_1 \equiv 2 x+y+z-1=0, P_2 \equiv x-y+z-2=0$ and $P_3 \equiv \alpha x-y+3 z-5=0$ intersect each other at point $P$ on XOY plane and at point $Q$ on YOZ plane, where $O$ is the origin then identify the correct statement(s)
options: straight line perpendicular to plane $P_3$ and passing through $P$ is $\frac{x-1}{4}=\frac{y+1}{-1}=\frac{z}{3}$
the length of projection of $\overrightarrow{P Q}$ on $x$-axis is 1
centroid of the triangle OPQ is $\left(\frac{1}{3},-\frac{1}{2}, \frac{1}{2}\right)$
questions.solution:
Three planes meet at two points it means they have infinite many solution, so
$\begin{aligned}
& \left|\begin{array}{ccc}
2 & 1 & 1 \\
1 & -1 & 1 \\
\alpha & -1 & 3
\end{array}\right|=0 \text { on solving } \alpha=4 \\
& \mathrm{P}_1: 2 x+y+z=1 \\
& \mathrm{P}_2: x-y+z=2 \\
& \mathrm{P}_3: 4 x-y+3 z=5 \\
& \mathrm{P}_{\text {on }} \mathrm{XOY} \text { plane } \equiv(1,-1,0) \text { (Which can be obtained by putting } z=0 \text { in any two equations) } \\
& Q \text { on } Y O Z \text { plane } \equiv\left(0,-\frac{1}{2}, \frac{3}{2}\right) \text { (By putting } x=0 \text { in any two equations) }
\end{aligned}$
questions.source: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced
questions.q: Which of the followings is/are correct :
options: The angle between the two straight lines $\vec{r}=3 \hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+4 \hat{k}+\lambda(-2 \hat{i}+\hat{j}+2 \hat{k})$ and $\vec{r}=\hat{i}+3 \hat{j}-2 \hat{k}+\mu(3 \hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+6 \hat{k})$ is $\cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{4}{21}\right)$

The force determined by the vector $\vec{r}=(1,-8,-7)$ is resolved along three mutually perpendicular directions, one of which is in the direction of the vector $\vec{a}=2 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+\hat{k}$. Then the vector component of the force $\vec{r}$ in the direction of the vector $\vec{a}$ is $-\frac{7}{3}(2 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+\hat{k})$
The cosine of the angle between any two diagonals of a cube is $\frac{1}{3}$.
questions.solution:
A : Angle between the given lines is = angle between the vectors $(-2 \hat{i}+\hat{j}+2 \hat{k})$ and $(3 \hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+6 \hat{k})$
$\Rightarrow \cos \theta=\frac{(-2 \hat{i}+\hat{j}+2 \hat{k}) \cdot(3 \hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+6 \hat{k})}{|-2 \hat{i}+\hat{j}+2 \hat{k}||3 \hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+6 \hat{k}|}=\cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{4}{21}\right)$
$B:$ Let $(\vec{r}=x \hat{i}+y \hat{j}+2 \hat{k})$ $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}}=x \hat{\mathrm{i}}+y \hat{\mathrm{j}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{k}}$
given expression is $x[y \hat{k}-z \hat{j}]+y[z \hat{i}-x \hat{k}]+z[x \hat{j}-y \hat{i}]=\overrightarrow{0}$
C: Component of $\vec{r}$ in direction of $\vec{a}=\frac{\vec{r} \cdot \vec{a}}{|\vec{a}|} \hat{a}$
$=\left\{\frac{(\hat{i}-8 \hat{j}-7 \hat{k}) \cdot(2 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+\hat{k})}{3}\right\} \frac{(2 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+\hat{k})}{3}=-\frac{21}{9}(2 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+\hat{k})=-\frac{7}{3}(2 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+\hat{k})$
D : Dr's of diagonal $\mathrm{BD}=\mathrm{a},-\mathrm{a}, \mathrm{a}$ or $1,-1,1$
Dr's of diagonal AF $=-\mathrm{a}, \mathrm{a}, \mathrm{a}$ or $-1,1,1$
Angle between above diagonals $\cos \theta=\left|\frac{-1-1+1}{\sqrt{3} \sqrt{3}}\right|=\frac{1}{3}$
questions.source: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced
questions.q: The direction cosines of the lines connected by the relations $1+m+n=0,21 m+2 \ln -\mathrm{mn}=0$ is/are
questions.solution:
Given lines are $\ell+\mathrm{m}+\mathrm{n}=0$    ....(1)
$2 \ell m+2 \ell n-m n=0$     ....(2)
Putting the value of n from (1) in (2), we get $2 \ell \mathrm{~m}+2 \ell\{-(\ell+\mathrm{m})\}-\mathrm{m}\{-(\ell+\mathrm{m})\}=0 \because \mathrm{n}=-(\ell+\mathrm{m})$
$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \mathrm{m}^2+\mathrm{m} \ell-2 \ell^2=0 \\ & \Rightarrow\left(\frac{\mathrm{~m}}{\mathrm{f}}\right)^2+\left(\frac{\mathrm{m}}{\ell}\right)-2=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{~m}}{\mathrm{f}}=-\frac{1 \pm \sqrt{1+8}}{2}=-\frac{1 \pm \sqrt{9}}{2}=-\frac{1 \pm 3}{2} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{~m}}{\ell}=-2.1\end{aligned}$
If $\frac{\mathrm{m}}{\mathrm{f}}=-2$    if $\frac{\mathrm{m}}{\ell}=1$
Then $\mathrm{n}=-(\ell-2 \ell)=\ell$      $\Rightarrow \mathrm{n}=-(2 \ell)$
$\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{m}}{-2}=\frac{\ell}{1}=\frac{\mathrm{n}}{1}$     $\frac{\mathrm{m}}{\mathrm{l}}=\frac{\ell}{1}=\frac{\mathrm{n}}{-2}$
$\therefore$ Direction cosines are $-\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{6}},-\frac{2}{\sqrt{6}}, \frac{1}{\sqrt{6}}\right):\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{6}}, \frac{1}{\sqrt{6}},-\frac{2}{\sqrt{6}}\right)$
questions.source: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced
questions.q: A plane meets the co-ordinates axes in $A, B, C$ such that the centroid of the triangle $A B C$ is the point $\left(1, r, r^2\right)$. If all such planes must pass through the point $(t, t, t)$ for all $r \in R-\{0\}$, then $t$ can satisfy
questions.solution:
Let the equation of the required plane be $\frac{x}{a}+\frac{y}{b}+\frac{z}{c}=1$
The points A, B, C are $(a, 0,0),(0, b, 0)$ and $(0,0, c)$ and the centroid of $\triangle A B C$ is $\left(\frac{a}{3}, \frac{b}{3}, \frac{c}{3}\right)$
Thus $a=3, b=3 r, c=3 r^2, r \neq 0$
So, the equation of plane is $\frac{x}{3}+\frac{y}{3 r}+\frac{z}{3 r^2}=1$
It passes through $(\mathrm{t}, \mathrm{t}, \mathrm{t})$
so, $(\mathrm{t}-3) \mathrm{r}^2+\mathrm{rt}+\mathrm{t}=0$, clearly $\mathrm{t} \neq 0$
for real $r, t^2-4(t-3) t \geq 0, t \neq 3 \Rightarrow 0 \lt t \leq 4, t \neq 3$
when $\mathrm{t}=3, \mathrm{r}=-1$, so $0 \lt \mathrm{t} \leq 4$
questions.source: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced
questions.q: A line $\frac{x-1}{2}=\frac{y-2}{3}=\frac{z-3}{4}$ intersects the plane $x-y+2 z+2=0$ at point $A$. The equation of the straight line passing through A lying in the given plane and at minimum inclination with the given line is/are
questions.solution:
$\begin{aligned} & 2 r+1-(3 r+2)+2(4 r+3)+2=0 \\ & \Rightarrow 7 r+7=0 \\ & \Rightarrow r=-1 \end{aligned}$ $\therefore \quad A(-1,-1,-1)$ required line will be projection of given line in the plane foot of $\perp$ of P will be on D $\frac{x-1}{1}=\frac{y-2}{-1}=\frac{z-3}{2}=-\left(\frac{1-2+2.3+2}{1^2+(-1)^2+2^2}\right)$ $\frac{x-1}{1}=\frac{y-2}{-1}=\frac{z-3}{2}=\frac{-7}{6}$
$x=\frac{-1}{6} ; y=\frac{19}{6} ; z=\frac{4}{6}$ $\Rightarrow \frac{x+1}{2}=\frac{y+1}{5}=\frac{z+1}{2} \quad \Rightarrow \frac{x+1}{2}+1=\frac{y+1}{5}+1=\frac{z+1}{2}+1$ Also, $\frac{x+1}{2}=\frac{y+1}{5}=\frac{z+1}{2}$ $\quad \Rightarrow \frac{x+1}{2}=\frac{y+1}{5}$ and $\frac{y+1}{5}=\frac{z+1}{2}$ $\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow 5 x-y+4=0=2 y-5 z-3 \\ & \text { also }(5 x-y+4)+(2 y-5 z-3)=0=2 y-5 z-3 \quad \Rightarrow 5 x+y-5 z+1=0=2 y-5 z-3\end{aligned}$
questions.source: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced
questions.q: Equation of the plane passing through $A\left(x_1, y_1, z_1\right)$ and containing the line $\frac{x-x_2}{d_1}=\frac{y-y_2}{d_2}=\frac{z-z_2}{d_3}$ is
options: $\left|\begin{array}{ccc}x-x_1 & y-y_1 & z-z_1 \\ x_2-x_1 & y_2-y_1 & z_2-z_1 \\ d_1 & d_2 & d_3\end{array}\right|=0$
$\left|\begin{array}{ccc}x-x_2 & y-y_2 & z-z_2 \\ x_1-x_2 & y_1-y_2 & z_1-z_2 \\ d_1 & d_2 & d_3\end{array}\right|=0$
$\left|\begin{array}{ccc}x & y & z \\ x_1-x_2 & y_1-y_2 & z_1-z_2 \\ d_1 & d_2 & d_3\end{array}\right|=0$
questions.solution:
Vectors $\overrightarrow{A R}, \overrightarrow{A B}$ \& $\vec{C}$ are coplanar Equation of the required plane $\left|\begin{array}{ccc}x-x_1 & y-y_1 & z-z_1 \\ x_2-x_1 & y_2-y_1 & z_2-z_1 \\ d_1 & d_2 & d_3\end{array}\right|=0$
or $\left|\begin{array}{ccc}x-x_2 & y-y_2 & z-z_2 \\ x_1-x_2 & y_1-y_2 & z_1-z_2 \\ d_1 & d_2 & d_3\end{array}\right|=0$
questions.source: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced
questions.q: If $\vec{a}, \vec{b}, \vec{c}$ and $\vec{d}$ are the position vectors of the points $A, B, C$ and $D$ respectively in three dimensional space no three of $A, B, C, D$ are collinear and satisfy the relation $3 \vec{a}-2 \vec{b}+\vec{c}-2 \vec{d}=\overrightarrow{0}$, then :
options: A, B, C and D are coplanar
The line joining the points $B$ and $D$ divides the line joining the point $A$ and $C$ in the ratio $2: 1$.
The line joining the points $A$ and $C$ divides the line joining the points $B$ and $D$ in the ratio $1: 1$.
The four vectors $\vec{a}, \vec{b}, \vec{c}$ and $\vec{d}$ are linearly dependents.
questions.solution:
$3 \vec{a}-2 \vec{b}+\vec{c}-2 \vec{d}=0$ sum of coefficient $=0$ $\Rightarrow \quad \vec{a}, \vec{b}, \vec{c}, \vec{d}$ are coplanar
Also $\quad 2 \vec{b}+2 \vec{d}=3 \vec{a}+\vec{c}$ $\Rightarrow \frac{\vec{b}+\vec{d}}{2}=\frac{3 \vec{a}+\vec{c}}{4}$
questions.source: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced
questions.q:
Consider the following three planes
$\begin{aligned}
& P_1: x=y \sin \alpha+z \sin \beta \\
& P_2: y=z \sin \theta+x \sin \alpha \\
& P_3: z=x \sin \beta+y \sin \theta
\end{aligned}$
If $P_1, P_2, P_3$ intersect in the line $\frac{x}{\cos \theta}=\frac{y}{\cos \beta}=\frac{z}{\cos \alpha}$ then the value of $\alpha+\beta+\theta$ is equal to
questions.solution:
If three planes pass through the same line, their normal vector will be coplanar
$\begin{aligned}
& \Rightarrow\left|\begin{array}{ccc}
-1 & \sin \alpha & \sin \beta \\
\sin \alpha & -1 & \sin \theta \\
\sin \beta & \sin \theta & -1
\end{array}\right|=0 \\
& \Rightarrow \sin ^2 \alpha+\sin ^2 \beta+\sin ^2 \theta+2 \sin \alpha \sin \beta \sin \theta=1
\end{aligned}$....(1)
Now the cross product $\hat{r}$ of normals of $P_1$, and $P_2$ will parallel to line
Direction ratio of $\hat{r}=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}\hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ -1 & \sin \alpha & \sin \beta \\ \sin \alpha & -1 & \sin \theta\end{array}\right|=\left\{(\sin \alpha \sin \theta+\sin \beta),(\sin \alpha \sin \beta+\sin \theta),\left(1-\sin ^2 \alpha\right)\right\}$ $\hat{r}$ is parallel to given line
$\Rightarrow \frac{\sin \alpha \sin \theta+\sin \beta}{\cos \theta}=\frac{\sin \alpha \sin \beta+\sin \theta}{\cos \beta}=\frac{1-\sin ^2 \alpha}{\cos \alpha}=\lambda$....(2)
Using (1) and (2) we get
$\lambda \cos \alpha=\lambda \sin (\beta+\theta) \Rightarrow \sin (\beta+\theta)=\sin \left(\frac{\pi}{2}-\alpha\right)=\alpha+\beta+\theta=2 n \pi+\frac{\pi}{2}(n \in z)$
questions.source: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced
questions.q: Let PM be the perpendicular from the point $\mathrm{P}(1,2,3)$ to xy plane, If $\overline{\mathrm{OP}}$ makes an angle O with the positive direction of z -axis and OM makes an angle $\phi$ with the positive direction of x -axis, where 0 is the origin and O and $\phi$ are acute angles, then
questions.solution:
If $\mathrm{P}(x, y, z)$ then the figure
$\begin{aligned} & x=r \sin \theta \sin \phi \\ & y=r \sin \theta \sin \phi \\ & z=r \cos \theta \\ & \Rightarrow 1=r \sin \theta \cos \phi \\ & 2=r \sin \theta \sin \phi \\ & 3=r \cos \theta \\ & \Rightarrow 1^2+2^2+3^3=r^2\end{aligned}$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{r}= \pm \sqrt{14}$
$\sin \theta \cos \phi=\frac{1}{\sqrt{14}}(\mathrm{r}=\sqrt{14} \because \theta \& \phi$ are acute $)$
$\sin \theta \sin \phi=\frac{2}{\sqrt{14}}$
$\cos \theta=\frac{3}{\sqrt{14}}$
$\therefore \frac{\sin \theta \sin \phi}{\sin \theta \cos \phi}=\frac{2}{1}$
$\Rightarrow \tan \phi=2$, Also $\tan \theta=\frac{\sqrt{5}}{3}$
questions.source: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced
questions.q: Let $L$ be a straight line passing through origin. Suppose that all the points on $L$ are at constant distance from the two planes $P_1: x+3 y-z+1=0$ and $P_2: 3 x-y+z-1=0$ then which of the following points lie(s) on the line $L$ :
questions.solution:
Clearly $L$ should be parallel to both the planes $P_1$ and $P_2$.
$\therefore \quad L$ is perpendicular to both normals $\overrightarrow{\mathbf{n}_1}=\hat{\mathbf{i}}+3 \hat{\mathbf{j}}-\hat{\mathbf{k}}$ and $\overrightarrow{\mathbf{n}_2}=3 \hat{\mathbf{i}}-\hat{\mathbf{j}}+\hat{\mathbf{k}}$
$\therefore \quad \overrightarrow{\mathbf{n}_1} \times \overrightarrow{\mathbf{n}_2}=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}\hat{\mathbf{i}} & \hat{\mathbf{j}} & \hat{\mathbf{k}} \\ 1 & 3 & -1 \\ 3 & -1 & 1\end{array}\right|=2 \hat{\mathbf{i}}-4 \hat{\mathbf{j}}-10 \hat{\mathbf{k}}$
$\therefore \quad$ Dr's of $L=(1,-2,-5)$
$\therefore \quad$ Equation of $L$ is $\frac{x}{1}=\frac{y}{-2}=\frac{z}{-5}$
$\therefore \quad$ Point $(1,-2,-5)$ and $(-1,2,5)$ lie on $L$.
questions.source: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced
questions.q: The line $x=2 y=3 z$ meets the plane $x+y+z=11$ at the point $P$ and the sphere centred at origin and radius equal to 14 , at the points $R$ and $S$, then -
questions.solution:
Any point on line $x=2 y=3 z$ is $\left(\lambda, \frac{\lambda}{2}, \frac{\lambda}{3}\right)$
line meets plane $x+y+z=11$ at $P$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{P}$ is $(6,3,2)$
Similarly it meets sphere given by
$x^2+y^2+z^2=196$ at $R \& S$
$\therefore  \lambda^2+\left(\frac{\lambda}{2}\right)^2+\left(\frac{\lambda}{3}\right)^2=196$
or $\lambda= \pm 12$
$\therefore  \mathrm{R}(12,6,4) \& S(-12,-6,-4)$
Now $P R=\sqrt{36+9+4}=7$,
$\mathrm{PS}=\sqrt{324+81+36}=21$
$\&  \mathrm{RS}=\sqrt{576+144+64}=28$
questions.source: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced
questions.q: If the coordinates of the point where the line $x-2 y+z-1=0=x+2 y-2 z-5$ intersects the plane $x+y-2 z=7$ is $(\alpha, \beta, \gamma)$, then:
questions.solution:
The required point of intersection of three planes
Solving eqns. (1), (2) and (3), we get $(1,-2,-4)$.
questions.source: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced
options: The line of intersection of planes $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{n}}_1=\mathrm{q}_1, \overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{n}}_2=\mathrm{q}_2$ and $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}} . \overrightarrow{\mathrm{n}}_3=\mathrm{q}_3, \overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{n}}_4=\mathrm{q}_4$ are perpendicular if $\left(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{n}}_1 \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{n}}_3\right)\left(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{n}}_2 \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{n}}_4\right)=\left(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{n}}_1 \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{n}}_4\right)\left(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{n}}_2 \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{n}}_3\right)$.

If three distinct planes $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}} . \vec{n}_1=q_1, \overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}} . \vec{n}_2=q_2, \overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}} . \overrightarrow{\mathrm{n}}_3=\mathrm{q}_3$ intersect in a line which is contained by the plane $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}} . \overrightarrow{\mathrm{n}}_4=\mathrm{q}_4$, then $\left[\begin{array}{lll}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{n}}_1 & \overrightarrow{\mathrm{n}}_2 & \overrightarrow{\mathrm{n}}_4\end{array}\right] \overrightarrow{\mathrm{n}}_3=\left[\begin{array}{lll}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{n}}_1 & \overrightarrow{\mathrm{n}}_2 & \overrightarrow{\mathrm{n}}_3\end{array}\right] \overrightarrow{\mathrm{n}}_4$.

If four distinct planes $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{n}}_1=\mathrm{q}_1, \overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}} . \overrightarrow{\mathrm{n}}_2=\mathrm{q}_2, \overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}} . \overrightarrow{\mathrm{n}}_3=\mathrm{q}_3$ and $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{n}}_4=\mathrm{q}_4$ intersect in a line, then $\left[\begin{array}{ccc}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{n}}_1 & \overrightarrow{\mathrm{n}}_2 & \overrightarrow{\mathrm{n}}_4\end{array}\right] \overrightarrow{\mathrm{n}}_3=\left[\begin{array}{lll}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{n}}_1 & \overrightarrow{\mathrm{n}}_2 & \overrightarrow{\mathrm{n}}_3\end{array}\right] \overrightarrow{\mathrm{n}}_4$

If a plane contains line of intersection of planes $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{n}}_1=\mathrm{q}_1, \overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{n}}_2=\mathrm{q}_2$ and is parallel to line of intersection of planes $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{n}}_3=\mathrm{q}_3, \overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{n}}_4=\mathrm{q}_4$ then $\left[\begin{array}{lll}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{n}}_1 & \overrightarrow{\mathrm{n}}_2 & \overrightarrow{\mathrm{n}}_4\end{array}\right] \overrightarrow{\mathrm{n}}_3=\left[\begin{array}{lll}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{n}}_1 & \overrightarrow{\mathrm{n}}_2 & \overrightarrow{\mathrm{n}}_3\end{array}\right] \overrightarrow{\mathrm{n}}_4$.
questions.solution:
(A) Line of intersection of $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{n}}_1=\mathrm{q}_1 \& \overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{n}}_2=\mathrm{q}_2$ is along $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{n}}_1 \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{n}}_2$.
line of intersection of
$\overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}} . \overrightarrow{\mathrm{n}}_3=\mathrm{q}_3 \& \overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}} . \overrightarrow{\mathrm{n}}_4=\mathrm{q}_4$ is along $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{n}}_3 \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{n}}_4$.
$\Rightarrow$ The two lines are perpendicular when
$\left(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{n}}_1 \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{n}}_2\right) \cdot\left(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{n}}_3 \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{n}}_4\right)=0$
$\Rightarrow\left(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{n}}_1 \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{n}}_3\right)\left(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{n}}_2 \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{n}}_4\right)-\left(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{n}}_1 \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{n}}_4\right)\left(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{n}}_2 \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{n}}_3\right)=0$
(B) $\left(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{n}}_1 \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{n}}_2\right) \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{n}}_4=0=\left(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{n}}_2 \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{n}}_3\right) \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{n}}_4=\left(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{n}}_1 \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{n}}_3\right) \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{n}}_4$
(C) $\left(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{n}}_1 \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{n}}_2\right) \times\left(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{n}}_3 \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{n}}_4\right)=0$
$\Rightarrow \quad\left[\begin{array}{lll}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{n}}_1 & \overrightarrow{\mathrm{n}}_2 & \overrightarrow{\mathrm{n}}_4\end{array}\right] \overrightarrow{\mathrm{n}}_3=\left[\begin{array}{lll}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{n}}_1 & \overrightarrow{\mathrm{n}}_2 & \overrightarrow{\mathrm{n}}_3\end{array}\right] \overrightarrow{\mathrm{n}}_4$
(D) Plane contains line along $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{n}}_1 \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{n}}_2$. Also plane is parallel to the line along $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{n}}_3 \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{n}}_4$.
But does not imply that $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{n}}_1 \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{n}}_2$ is parallel to $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{n}}_3 \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{n}}_4$.
questions.source: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced
questions.q: If three planes $P_1 \equiv 2 x+y+z-1=0, P_2=x-y+z-2=0$ and $P_3 \equiv \alpha x-y+3 z-5=0$ intersects each other at point $P$ on $X O Y$ plane and at point $Q$ on $Y O Z$ plane, where $O$ is the origin then identify the correct statement(s)?
options: Straight line perpendicular to plane $P_3$ and passing through $P$ is $\frac{x-1}{4}=\frac{y+1}{-1}=\frac{z}{3}$.
The length of projection of $\overrightarrow{\mathbf{P Q}}$ on $x$-axis is 1 .
Centroid of the triangle $O P Q$ is $\left(\frac{1}{3}, \frac{-1}{2}, \frac{1}{2}\right)$
questions.solution:
Three planes meet at two points it means they have infinitely many solutions, so
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \qquad\left|\begin{array}{ccc}
2 & 1 & 1 \\
1 & -1 & 1 \\
\alpha & -1 & 3
\end{array}\right|=0 \\
& \Rightarrow 2(-3+1)-1(3+1)+\alpha(1+1)=0 . \quad \Rightarrow \quad \alpha=4 \\
& P_1: 2 x+y+z=1 \\
& P_2: x-y+z=2 \\
& P_3: 4 x-y+3 z=5 \\
& P \text { on } X O Y \text { plane }=(1,-1,0)
\end{aligned}
$$
(which can be obtained by putting $z=0$ in any two of the given planes.)
$$
Q \text { on } Y O Z \text { plane } \equiv\left(0, \frac{-1}{2}, \frac{3}{2}\right)
$$
(which can be obtained by putting $x=0$ in any two of the given planes.)
$\therefore \quad$ Straight line perpendicular to plane $P_3$ passing through $P$ is :
$$
\begin{gathered}
\frac{x-1}{4}=\frac{y+1}{-1}=\frac{z}{3} \\
\overrightarrow{\mathbf{P Q}}=\hat{\mathbf{i}}-\frac{1}{2} \hat{\mathbf{j}}-\frac{3}{2} \hat{\mathbf{k}} \\
\rightarrow\left|\frac{\overrightarrow{\mathrm{OP}} \cdot \hat{\mathbf{i}}}{|\hat{\mathbf{i}}|}\right|=1
\end{gathered}
$$
Centroid of $\triangle O P Q$ is $\left(\frac{1}{3}, \frac{-1}{2}, \frac{1}{2}\right)$.
questions.source: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced
questions.q: Equation of the plane passing through the line of intersection of the two planes $\vec{r} \cdot \vec{n}_1=q_1$ and $\vec{r} \cdot \vec{n}_2=q_2$ and parallel to the line of intersection of $\vec{r} \cdot \vec{n}_3=q_3$ and $\vec{r} \cdot \vec{n}_4=q_4$ is
questions.solution:
Equation of the required plane is
$\left(\overline{\mathrm{r}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{n}_1}-\mathrm{q}_1\right)+\lambda\left(\overline{\mathrm{r}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{n}_2}-\mathrm{q}_2\right)=0 \Rightarrow \overline{\mathrm{r}} \cdot\left(\overline{\mathrm{n}_1}+\lambda \overline{\mathrm{n}_2}\right)=\left(\mathrm{q}_1+\lambda \mathrm{q}_2\right)$

Now, perpendicular to $\bar{n}_3 \times \mathrm{n}_4^{-}$
$\begin{aligned}
& \left(\overline{\mathrm{n}}_1+\lambda \overline{\mathrm{n}_2}\right) \cdot\left(\overline{\mathrm{n}_3} \times \overline{\mathrm{n}_4}\right)=0 \\
& \lambda=-\frac{\left[\overline{\mathrm{n}_1} \overline{\mathrm{n}_3} \overline{\mathrm{n}_4}\right]}{\left[\overline{\mathrm{n}_2} \overline{\mathrm{n}_3} \overline{\mathrm{n}_4}\right]}
\end{aligned}$

So independent on $\mathrm{q}_3$ and $\mathrm{q}_4$ only.
questions.source: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced
questions.q: A rod of length 2 units whose one end is $(1,0,-1)$ and other end touches the plane $x-2 y+2 z+4=0$, is rotated on this plane, then
options: the rod sweeps a solid structure whose volume is $\pi$ cubic units
the area of the region which the rod traces on the plane is $2 \pi$
the length of projection of the rod on the plane is $\sqrt{3}$ units
the centre of the region which the rod traces on the plane is $\left(\frac{2}{3}, \frac{2}{3}, \frac{-5}{3}\right)$
questions.solution: The rod sweeps out the figure which is a cone. The distance of point $(A(1,0,-1)$ from the plane is $\frac{1+1-2+4 \mid}{\sqrt{9}}=1$ unit. The slant height / of the cone is 2 units. Then the radius of the base of the cone is $\sqrt{l^2-1}=\sqrt{4-1}=\sqrt{3}$ Hence, the volume of the cone is $\frac{\pi}{3}(\sqrt{3})^2 .1=\pi$ cubic units. Area of the circle on the plane which the rod traces is $3 \pi$. Also, the centre of the circle is $Q(x, y, z)$. Then $\begin{aligned} & \frac{x-1}{1}=\frac{y-0}{-2}=\frac{z+1}{2}=\frac{-(1-0-2+4)}{1^2+(-2)^2+2^2} \\ & Q(x, y, z) \equiv\left(\frac{2}{3}, \frac{2}{3}, \frac{-5}{3}\right)\end{aligned}$
questions.source: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced
questions.q: $D-A B C$ is a tetrahedron with $A=(2,0,0), B=(0,4,0)$ and $C D=\sqrt{14}$. Edge $C D$ lies on the line $\frac{x-1}{1}=\frac{y-1}{2}=\frac{z-3}{3}$. If locus of centroid of tetrahedron is $\frac{x-\frac{3}{2}}{1}=\frac{y-y_1}{a}=\frac{z-z_1}{b}$, then which of the following is/are true:
questions.solution:
Let $C=\left(\lambda_1+1,2 \lambda_1+2,3 \lambda_1+3\right)$ and $D=\left(\lambda_2+1,2 \lambda_2+2,3 \lambda_2+3\right)$
$\therefore \quad C D=\sqrt{14}\left(\lambda_1-\lambda_2\right)=\sqrt{14} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \lambda_1=\lambda_2+1$
$\therefore \quad$ Locus is $\frac{4 x-5}{2}=\frac{4 y-10}{4}=\frac{4 z-9}{6}$
$\therefore \quad$ Dr's of line $1,2,3$ and point $\left(\frac{3}{2}, 3,3\right)$ satisfies the line
$\therefore \quad$ Locus is $\frac{x-\frac{3}{2}}{1}=\frac{y-3}{3}=\frac{z-3}{3}$
questions.source: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced
